query: definition of antiphlogastic and its role in inflammation

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Eluting Stent Compounds -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Problems with hypersensitivity to stents have been proposed and subsequently, the concept of polymer-free or biodegradable polymers was introduced. Recently reestablishment of healthy long-term vasomotion with biodegradable stents have gained significance.

retrieved_docs.title: Buerger Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is no definitive etiology of Buerger disease, yet tobacco exposure is required for both disease initiation and progression. The mechanism of the disease remains a mystery, but it may involve immunologic dysfunction and tobacco hypersensitivity that is associated with enhanced cellular sensitivity to type-1 and type-3 collagen, impaired endothelium-dependent vasorelaxation, and increased anti-endothelial cell antibody titers. A genetic link may exist as affected subjects have an increased prevalence of human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-A9, HLA-B5, and HLA-54.

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- History and Physical -- Classification Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: The 1997 ACR criteria required 4 out of 11 criteria for the classification of SLE. The 11 criteria included were malar rash, discoid rash, photosensitivity, alopecia, Raynaud phenomenon, oral/nasal ulcers, arthritis (non-erosive arthritis involving 2 or more peripheral joints), serositis (pleurisy or pericarditis), renal disease (proteinuria greater than 500 mg daily or cellular RBC, granular, tubular, or mixed casts), hematologic disease (hemolytic anemia with reticulocytosis, or leukopenia less than 4000/mm3 on 2 or more occasions or lymphopenia less than 1500/mm3 on 2 or more occasions, or thrombocytopenia less than 100,000/mm3 in the absence of medications known to decrease platelets), neurologic disease (seizures or psychosis in the absence of an alternative explanation), immunologic criteria (Antiphospholipid antibodies present based on either an abnormal serum level of IgM or IgG anticardiolipin antibodies or a tested positive result for lupus anticoagulant or anti-DNA antibody or anti-Sm antibody or false-positive syphilis test with VDRL or RPR) and antinuclear antibody positivity in the absence of drugs known to cause drug-induced lupus.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Eluting Stent Compounds -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Different characteristics make each drug-eluting stent unique. Each of them has advantages and disadvantages which derive from differences in drug-loading capacity, drug-release pharmacokinetics, polymer durability, biocompatibility, influence on vascular wall thinning, aneurysm formation, and delayed restenosis. [22] Different complications can occur following the implantation of stents. Materials not native to the human body involve questions of biocompatibility. Problems of corrosion and release of toxic substances led to the investigation of more biocompatible and degradable compounds. Early biocompatibility problems include stent thrombosis, inflammation, and neointima formation. A late problem associated with decreased biocompatibility is scaffold fracture due to material fatigue.

retrieved_docs.title: C-Reactive Protein: Clinical Relevance and Interpretation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: CRP functions as an acute-phase reactant, primarily induced by interleukin 6 acting on the hepatic gene responsible for CRP transcription during inflammatory or infectious processes. Studies have explored the potential link between CRP dysregulation in the clearance of apoptotic cells and cellular debris and the onset of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), though definitive evidence is currently lacking. Animal models of alveolitis have demonstrated protective effects of CRP in lung tissue, where it reduces neutrophil-mediated alveolar damage and protein leakage into the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Drug-Induced Interstitial Nephritis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: DI-AIN can be definitively diagnosed only by renal biopsy, characterized by an inflammatory infiltrate in the renal interstitium and tubules that typically does not involve the glomeruli. [7] [15] [41] Light microscopy shows interstitial inflammation with a predominance of T-lymphocytes, both CD4+ and CD8+, along with eosinophils, whose presence near the tubular basement membrane is diagnostic of AIN. Neutrophils and plasma cells may also be present. The inflammatory infiltrate is typically associated with interstitial edema and tubulitis. An altered brush border, prominent nucleoli, cytoplasmic vacuolization, tubular dilation, and disruption of the tubular basement membrane characterize Tubulitis. Immunofluorescence will usually not show a specific immunoglobulin or complement pattern, and electron microscopy is useful in diagnosing NSAID-induced AIN, causing a minimal change in disease. [43] [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Kidney Transplant Rejection -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: CKTR is, by definition, immune-mediated and generally divides into chronic active antibody-mediated rejection (CAAMR) and chronic active T cell-mediated rejection (CATMR). [7] CAAMR occurs due to DSA against human leukocytic antigens (HLA) and non-HLA antigens. DSAs can damage the endothelium both directly and indirectly through complement-mediated activation and inflammatory cell recruitment. An extended alloreactive immune response over a prolonged period leads to microvascular remodeling of both the glomerular and peritubular capillaries, microvascular inflammation, and arterial intimal fibrous thickening. Complement activation, identified by C4d deposition in the peritubular capillaries, also contributes to microvascular inflammation. However, C4d positivity was eliminated as a requirement for the diagnosis of CAAMR after the emergence of C4d negative antibody-mediated kidney rejection. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome -- Introduction -- Historical Evolution of Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: The goal of the initial definition was to be highly sensitive using easily available parameters across all healthcare settings. An unavoidable corollary of such a definition was, therefore, the lack of specificity. Additional limitations of the SIRS definition include the following: The universal prevalence of the parameters in an ICU setting Lack of ability to distinguish between beneficial host response from pathologic host response that contributes to organ dysfunction Distinguishing between infectious and noninfectious etiology purely based on the definition Lack of weight to each criterion, eg, fever and elevated respiratory rate, has precisely the same significance as leukocytosis or tachycardia by the SIRS definition. Inability to predict organ dysfunction [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Weibel-Palade Bodies -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many other protein molecules are stored in Weibel-Palade bodies, including interleukin 8, endothelin 1, eotaxin-3, osteoprotegerin, angiopoietin-2, and alpha-1,3-fucosyltransferase VI. These proteins serve as mediators of inflammation, immune response, angiogenesis, and regulation of vessel caliber in response to stressors.

retrieved_docs.title: Quincke Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Quincke sign is named after Dr. Heinrich Quincke, a German physician who first coined the term in 1868. [7] Dr. Quincke also contributed to many other notable medical discoveries, including the identification of angioedema, the technique of the lumbar puncture, and the description of what is now known as idiopathic intracranial hypertension. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Reactive Arthritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goal of therapy in reactive arthritis is to provide symptomatic relief and prevent chronic complications. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are the initial treatment of choice in the acute phase. Intra-articular or local glucocorticoids, as in cases of enthesitis or bursitis, can be used if the patient has mono/oligoarthritis. Mechanical devices like orthotics and insoles can be useful. Systemic use of glucocorticoids is limited to severe polyarthritis and cardiac and ocular manifestations. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs, mainly sulphasalazine, are effective in both acute and chronic ReA. Other agents, such as methotrexate and azathioprine, are useful in chronic arthritis. They are indicated in patients who have failed Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) therapy. Biologicals such as tumor necrosis factor blocking agents (eg, infliximab and etanercept have been suggested in treating reactive arthritis. However, further studies are needed to determine their definitive indications. [18] [19] [20] All patients should be urged to become physically active. Strengthening exercises are a key component of long-term therapy to prevent muscle wasting.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimecrolimus -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: TCIs such as pimecrolimus have a black-box warning from theoretical risks based on high dose systemic calcineurin inhibitor use in post-transplant patients and animal studies. [19] There is an increased risk of infections, lymphoma, and skin malignancies in those study populations that correlates positively with the dosage and duration of systemic immunosuppression. Although there has been no definite establishment of a causal relationship, skin malignancies and lymphomas have rarely been reported in patients using topical pimecrolimus. Patients or parents should be made aware of the black-box warning but reassured that the risk of malignancy is low when adhering to the proper use of topical calcineurin inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Opportunistic pathogens such as Mycobacterium , fungi, viruses, and parasites can cause latent or subacute infections in HIV/AIDS patients. Due to an immunodeficient state and lack of inflammatory response, the patient can remain asymptomatic. However, after HAART initiation, an improvement in an immune-mediated inflammatory response is anticipated. This hyper-inflammatory response is the hallmark of IRIS. The clinical presentation depends on the underlying pathogen and organ/system involved and the severity of the inflammatory response. [30] There is no universal definition of IRIS or broad consensus on diagnostic criteria. Still, the following criteria usually need to be met before the diagnosis of IRIS in HIV-positive patients:

retrieved_docs.title: Alternative Medicine -- Definition/Introduction -- Herbal Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: Many case reports have been documented describing the beneficial effects of herbs and Traditional Chinese Medicine, claiming cures for various diseases. Several large-scale evaluations have been proposed to validate these claims and improve the legitimacy of herbal substances within the scope of Western medicine. Some commonly used herbal supplements include: Black cohosh: Primarily used for issues regarding the female reproductive system, such as menstrual cramps Echinacea: Used to enhance the immune system Garlic: Noted for its beneficial cardiovascular effects, particularly cholesterol Ginseng: A commonly used energy-boosting agent (often found in energy drinks) St Johns’ Wort: Claims purport that it improves mood, particularly mild-to-moderate depression

retrieved_docs.title: Calcineurin Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Pimecrolimus
retrieved_docs.content: The prolonged safety of topical CNI formulations has not been definitively established. Consequently, continuous and long-term topical application of CNIs, such as pimecrolimus, should be avoided. If necessary, their application should be restricted to the affected areas associated with atopic dermatitis. Malignancy: Although no definitive causal link has been established, there are infrequent reports of malignancies, such as lymphoma and skin malignancies, in patients using the topical pimecrolimus formulation. Pediatric patients: Pimecrolimus is not approved by the FDA for usage in children younger than 2.

retrieved_docs.title: Xenon -- Definition/Introduction -- Xenon in Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: Organ protection: An important area of research is Xe-induced organ protection. For instance, Xe has been proposed as a means to prevent ischemia or reperfusion damage following Stanford type-A acute aortic dissection surgery. [4] Several preclinical investigations conducted on various models subjected to preconditioning, real-time conditioning, and postconditioning have demonstrated that Xe may provide significant neuroprotective and cardioprotective effects. These effects are dose-dependent and are thought to result from Xe's interference with glutamatergic transmission, as glutamate receptors are involved in both anesthesia and acute neurological injury through apoptotic processes, as well as its ability to inhibit the inflammatory cascade. [5] [6]

Overall, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of drug classes—linking anti‑inflammatory propionic‑acid derivatives, lipid‑lowering bile acid sequestrants, and glucose‑lowering agents—while also emphasizing the importance of accurate classification and awareness of contraindications to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 234
retrieved_docs.content: important contraindications: it should not be used in patients with الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية or in رضيع (infants), highlighting the need for caution in autoimmune and pediatric populations. Moreover, methoxsalen may be treated by الفطريات الفطرية (fungal agents), suggesting that fungal infection can affect its therapeutic use or that antifungal strategies may be required when methoxsalen is employed. Overall, these interconnected concepts illustrate how gastrointestinal inflammation, hematologic abnormalities, autoimmune disease, infectious status, and specific pharmacologic agents are interrelated in clinical practice, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and careful selection of therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2214
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Terminology encompasses a range of concepts relevant to the disease, including the antiretroviral drug ritonavir and the steroidal compound dehydroepiandrosteron. Dehydroepiandrosteron is classified as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, indicating that it belongs to the broader class of anti‑inflammatory steroids and may play a role in modulating inflammatory processes. By incorporating both ritonavir, which targets viral replication, and dehydroepiandrosteron, which can affect inflammation, the NCIt COVID-19 Terminology reflects the dual therapeutic focus on antiviral activity and control of the inflammatory response in the management of COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory steroids and dehydroepiandrosterone represent separate classes of steroid compounds; anti‑inflammatory steroids are not a subtype of dehydroepiandrosterone. While anti‑inflammatory steroids are synthetic glucocorticoids used therapeutically to suppress inflammation, dehydroepiandrosterone is an endogenous adrenal hormone with distinct metabolic and hormonal functions. Recognizing that these agents belong to different steroid families is important for accurate classification and appropriate clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 378
retrieved_docs.content: Beklomethason and flutikason are both classified as locally acting corticosteroids and belong to the potent (group III) subclass of corticosteroids. As members of these corticosteroid groups, each drug functions as an antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents used to reduce inflammation in targeted tissues. The categorization of both beklomethason and flutikason under “Corticosteroids acting locally,” “Corticosteroids, potent (group III),” and “antiflogistika” highlights their shared pharmacologic profile as potent, locally applied anti‑inflammatory corticosteroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1566
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excl. corticosteroids, are classified as a specific type of intestinal antiinflammatory agents. This subclass of drugs combines antiallergic activity with intestinal anti‑inflammatory effects while deliberately excluding corticosteroid compounds, distinguishing them from broader anti‑inflammatory therapies that contain steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 472
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology and GDC Value Terminology are medical classification systems that each encompass a range of therapeutic agents, anatomical structures, disease entities, and procedural conditions. Both terminologies include the angiotensin‑receptor neprilysin inhibitor sacubitril/valsartan, the cyclo‑oxygenase‑2 selective non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug celecoxib, and the glucocorticoid prednisolone (prednison). They also contain the condition described in Arabic as “ناسور السبيل التناسلي لدى الإناث,” referring to a female genital tract fistula. Vascular anatomy is represented by the concept “arterie,” and pharmacologic inhibition of gastric acid secretion is covered by the class “inhibitory protonové pumpy,” denoting proton‑pump inhibitors. Additionally, the antiplatelet agent nitroaspirin and the infectious condition septicemia (except in labor) are included. The parallel inclusion of these concepts in both GDC Terminology and GDC Value Terminology highlights a shared scope that integrates cardiovascular drugs, anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapies, gastrointestinal pharmacology, vascular structures, obstetric‑related infections, and specific female reproductive disorders, facilitating comprehensive documentation and analysis across diverse clinical domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the mesalamin group and function as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excluding corticosteroids, are also categorized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Additionally, corticosteroids acting locally are recognized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Together, these drug classes—mesalamin derivatives, antiallergic non‑corticosteroid agents, and locally acting corticosteroids—share the therapeutic role of reducing inflammation within the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting the diverse pharmacologic approaches available for managing intestinal inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1660
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excl. corticosteroids, and corticosteroids acting locally are both classified as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. These two categories represent distinct subgroups within the broader class of intestinal antiinflammatory agents, highlighting the diversity of therapeutic options used to manage intestinal inflammation. Recognizing that antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs (excluding corticosteroids) and locally acting corticosteroids each belong to this class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory treatments based on the specific pharmacologic properties and intended use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: Butylpyrazolidines, antiinflammatory and antirheumatic drugs, are classified as a type of non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic product.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 936
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Terminology incorporates several specific concepts, including the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazol, the antiviral agent ritonavir, and the steroid precursor dehydroepiandrosteron. Both omeprazol and the serotonergic agent flibanserin are classified under the broader chemical class benzimidazole, indicating that they are specific members of this heterocyclic family. A related term, benzimidazoly, is mapped from flibanserin, suggesting it represents a synonymous or derived concept within the same chemical context. Dehydroepiandrosteron is further placed within the category of anti‑inflammatory steroids, reflecting its role as a steroidal compound with anti‑inflammatory properties. Together, these relationships illustrate how individual drugs and biologically active molecules are organized within larger pharmacologic and terminological frameworks, which is important for accurate classification, research, and patient‑care considerations in the context of COVID‑19 and related therapeutic areas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1796
retrieved_docs.content: The CTRP Agent Terminology encompasses several pharmacologic agents, specifically domperidon, famotidin, and dehydroepiandrosteron. Dehydroepiandrosteron is additionally classified as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, indicating its role in reducing inflammation. Thus, dehydroepiandrosteron belongs both to the CTRP Agent Terminology and to the broader class of anti‑inflammatory steroids, while domperidon and famotidin are included in the terminology without further subclassification. Recognizing these relationships helps organize these agents for research, clinical reference, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 449
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology is a classification framework that incorporates a range of distinct medical concepts, including the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug celecoxib, the class of medications known as inhibitory proton pumps (commonly referred to as proton‑pump inhibitors), and the infectious condition septicemia, specifically excluding cases that occur in the context of labor. By grouping these diverse entities—an analgesic/anti‑inflammatory agent, a drug class that reduces gastric acid secretion, and a serious systemic infection—GDC Terminology provides a structured reference for clinicians and researchers to identify and discuss each concept within a unified terminology system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1604
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology is a classification system that includes the pharmacologic agents sacubitril / valsartan, celekoxib, and inhibitory protonové pumpy, as well as the clinical condition septicemia (except in labor). By grouping these distinct concepts—an angiotensin‑receptor neprilysin inhibitor combination, a COX‑2 selective non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug, a class of acid‑suppressing medications, and a severe systemic infection not related to childbirth—GDC Terminology provides a unified framework for organizing both therapeutic agents and disease entities, supporting comprehensive documentation and decision‑making in patient care.
